A patient has an chest CT with a scan range of 20 cm.
If the scan is repeated with a scan range of 30 cm.
What will happen to the equivalent dose?
explanation:
Equival[e]nt dose (in Siev[e]rt or r[e]m) is the
product of the absorbed dose and the radiation
weighting factor. CT uses x-ray photons with a
radiation weighting factor of 1. Since this will not
change by increasing the scan range, the
equivalent dose will not change.

question_text:
A patient has an chest CT with a scan range of 20 cm.
If the scan is repeated with a scan range of 30 cm.
What will happen to the effective dose?
explanation:
Increasing the scan range will irradiate more
tissues. Since the effective dose sums the product
of the absorbed dose to different tissues and tissue
sensitivity to radiation (i.e. tissue weighting factor),
the effective dose will increase as the scan range
increases. Effective dose is used when discussing
radiation risks.

question_text: What happens in CT fluoroscopy?
choices: A. Both the table and the tube are stationary
B. Table moves continuously but the tube is stationary
C. Table is stationary but the tube moves continuously
D. Both the table and the tube move continuously
correct_answer_text: C. Table is stationary but the tube moves continuously
explanation:
Data is collected in one plane, so the table remains
fixed. Images are updated continuously so the tube
and detectors rotate continuously to obtain
constantly updated projection views.

question_text:
Regarding automated exposure control techniques in
CT, most techniques:
choices: A. Work optimally for step-and-shoot acquisitions
B. Work best with fixed mA techniques
C. Result in average radiation dose savings of approximately 60% compared to manual techniques
D. Rely on modulation of the tube current
E. Rely on modulation of the kV
correct_answer_text: D. Rely on modulation of the tube current
explanation:
Automatic tube current modulation is the most
widespread form of AEC available on CT scanners.
This technique adjusts the tube current as the
patient moves through the gantry aperture (z
modulation) and/or as the tube rotates around the
patient (xy modulation).

question_text:
Which of the following statements is false regarding
prospective ECG triggering acquisition?
choices: A. It is a helical acquisition
B. Data is acquired for only a portion of the cardiac cycle
C. Cardiac function (e.g. left ventricular ejection fraction) cannot be calculated
D. Radiation dose is usually decreased compared to retrospective ECG gating
correct_answer_text: A. It is a helical acquisition
explanation:
It is an axial acquisition, not a helical acquisition.
Other statements are true. The x-ray beam is on
continuously with retrospective ECG triggering
acquisition to collect data throughout the cardiac
cycle, as compared to only one portion of the
cardiac cycle for prospective. Therefore, radiation
dose with prospective ECG gating is usually
decreased compared to retrospective. However,
cardiac function cannot be calculated.

question_text:
Which of the following statements is false regarding
multi-segment reconstruction in cardiac CT exams?
choices: A. It can be used in patients with major cardiac arrhythmias
B. Projection data selected from various parts of the cardiac cycle are used to generate any single image of the heart
C. It yields better temporal resolution than partial scan reconstruction
correct_answer_text: A. It can be used in patients with major cardiac arrhythmias
explanation:
In multiple-segment reconstruction, the scan
projection data required to perform a partial scan
reconstruction are selected from various sequential
cardiac cycles. This is possible only with
retrospective gating and a steady heart rhythm.
Other statements are true.

question_text:
What is the fundamental radiation dose parameter
measured in CT?
explanation:
CTDI is the fundamental radiation dose parameter
measured in CT. The CTDI is a measure of the
dose to a standard phantom (16 cm diameter acrylic
cylinder for a head and 32 cm diameter acrylic
cylinder for a body) under specific settings (kVp,
mAs, pitch, etc). It is important to realize that CTDI
is a dose index, and it is not the dose to a patient,
i.e. the patient is not made of plastic. Relative
changes in CTDI do reflect the relative changes in
patient dose however. DLP is calculated from
CTDIvol and scan length. In contrast, the size
specific dose estimate (SSDE) can be calculated
from the CTDI and the patient size (e.g., effective
diameter), and it is a measure of the average dose
within a slice of a specific patient.

question_text: What is the most common kVp used in CT scanners?
explanation:
100-150 is the most common kVp range used in CT
scanners. Specifically, most scans are performed at
100 kVp, 120 kVp or 140 kVp.

question_text:
Each of the following is true of breast shielding
except:
choices: B. Absorbs some of the signal for PA projections
D. Has no effect when dose modulation utilized
correct_answer_text: D. Has no effect when dose modulation utilized
explanation:
If the breast shield is applied to the patient after the
scout view is acquired, the z-axis auto mA
modulation will not be affected and the shield will
reduce dose to the breast; if it is applied to the
patient before the scout view is acquired, the z-axis
auto mA modulation will increase the dose to
compensate for the shield. The transverse (x,y)
modulation depends on the method employed by
the CT scanning system, and in some cases, the
shield will cause the transverse modulation to
increase the dose to compensate for the shield if it
is applied after the scout image is acquired.

question_text: The raw data of a CT scan:
choices: A. Is typically smaller (fewer Mbytes) than the reconstructed image data
B. Looks like a blurry image of the patient
C. Contains the values of x-ray detector response from all views and rays within a scan
D. Includes the image data reconstructed at varying slice thicknesses
correct_answer_text: C. Contains the values of x-ray detector response from all views and rays within a scan
explanation:
The raw data contains the values of x-ray detector
response from all views and rays within a scan prior
to reconstruction. It is much larger in size than the
reconstructed volumetric image data. Only after
reconstruction are there any data resembling the
patient.

question_text: What do gray scale Doppler images represent?
explanation:
Gray scale images are created using the amplitude
of the returning echoes. Color images in Doppler
use the frequencies of the returning echoes to
assign colors and measure velocities.

question_text: What is the resistive index?
choices: C. (peak systole - end diastole)/(1 - end diastole)
explanation:
Resistive index (Rl) = (peak systole - end
diastole)/peak systole. Arteries supplying organs
have lower RI's than arteries suppling the
extremities/muscles. Veins have resistive indexes
near O because there is little difference in flow
velocities between systole and diastole. Arteries
supplying organs have resistive indexes around 0.8
because there is continued perfusion to organs
during diastole. If the Rl is 1, then there is no
diastolic flow. If the RI is greater than 1, then there
is reversed flow during diastole (such as in renal
vein thrombosis).

question_text:
Increasing the ultrasound gain will result in which of
the following?
choices: B. Greater signal to noise ratio
explanation:
Gain is applied to the returning echo and does not
affect the emitted ultrasound wave. By increasing or
decreasing gain the overall image brightness
increases or decrease respectively. The SNR and
resolution remains constant. Increasing transmit
power changes the beam and results in increased
depth.

question_text:
What causes the hyperechogenicity deep to a simple
cyst?
explanation:
Through transmission or posterior acoustic
enhancement is seen deep to a simple cyst and
must be seen for a cyst to be described as simple.

question_text: Which substance has the highest sound attenuation?
explanation:
Gas results in the greatest attenuation to sound
waves. This is why ultrasound is not used to
evaluate the lung parenchyma. The correct order
from highest to lower attenuation is as follows: Gas,
bone, muscle and finally fluid.

question_text:
Curvilinear transducer arrays are used when imaging
which of the following structure?
explanation:
Curvilinear transducers provide a large FOV and
are used to image deep structures such as needed
during abdominal or pelvic ultrasounds. Curvilinear
transducers have lower frequency/spatial
resolution, higher penetration than linear
transducers.

question_text:
Time Gain Compensation allows for the adjustment of
gain at different ?
explanation:
Time Gain Compensation (TGC) allow the user to
adjust gain according to image depth. For example,
if the ultrasound beam is being attenuated by a fatty
liver, the deep portion of the liver will be poorly
visualized. Increasing the TGC in this circumstance
will make the liver appear more uniform.

question_text:
What occurs with higher frequency ultrasound
waves?
explanation:
As wavelength decreases, frequency increases
resulting in more scatter and therefore beam
attenuation. As a result, high frequencies have
decreased tissue penetration but increased spatial
resolution. Amplitude is independent of wavelength.

question_text: What occurs with decreased ultrasound wavelength?
explanation:
As wavelength decreases, frequency increases
resulting in more scatter and therefore beam
attenuation. As a result, high frequencies have
decreased tissue penetration but increased spatial
resolution. Amplitude is independent of wavelength.

question_text:
When should spectral Doppler ultrasound not be
performed on pregnant women?
explanation:
Because spectral Doppler ultrasound has higher
energy (higher thermal and mechanical indexes), it
should not be used during the first trimester. This
statement is somewhat controversial because the
effects of using spectral Doppler during the 1st
trimester are largely unknown.

question_text:
As ultrasound image depth increases, how is the
frame rate affected?
explanation:
Frame Rate = PRF/N = c/(2DN), where PRF =
pulsed repetition frequency, N = scan lines per
frame, c = speed of sound in material, D = depth of
image. Frame rates decrease inversely with depth
and scan lines/frame. It's easiest to think of this
intuitively. It takes longer to acquire an image with
greater depth or better lateral resolution (more scan
lines/frame). If it takes longer, the next image has to
wait for the first to finish which decreases frame rate
or temporal resolution.

question_text:
In ultrasound, the mechanical index (Ml) indicates
which of the following?
choices: A. Maximum temperature rise at standard parameters
B. Likelihood of cell membrane destruction
C. Likelihood of streaming or cavitation
D. Maximum temperature rise at current parameters
correct_answer_text: C. Likelihood of streaming or cavitation
explanation:
The mechanical index (Ml) is a measure of the
likelihood of mechanical bioeffects, i.e. streaming
and cavitation. When scanning pregnant women, a
risk-benefit decision should be made when the MI is
greater than 0.5.

question_text:
In ultrasound, the thermal index (Tl) indicates which
of the following?
choices: B. Maximum temperature rise at current parameters
D. Maximum temperature rise at standard parameters
correct_answer_text: B. Maximum temperature rise at current parameters
explanation:
The thermal index (TI) indicates the maximum
temperature rise at current scanning parameters.
When scanning pregnant women, a risk-benefit
decision should be made when the TI is greater
than 1.0.

question_text:
Linear transducer arrays are used when imaging
which of the following structure?
explanation:
Linear transducers have higher frequency/spatial
resolution, lower penetration than curvilinear
transducers. They are used to image superficial
structures and small parts and are typically used to
image vessels and the musculoskeletal system.
Higher frequency (linear) transducers also have
increased energy deposition and a greater
sensitivity in detecting slow flow.

question_text:
Compared to color Doppler, which of the following is
a unique feature of power Doppler?
choices: C. Less sensitive in detecting slow flow
explanation:
Power Doppler is more sensitive in detecting slow
flow and can be of value in differentiating thrombus
from slow flow. Power Doppler cannot be used to
determine flow direction or velocity. Aliasing is seen
with color Doppler. Flash artifact is seen with power
Doppler because it is more sensitive to transducer
motion.

question_text:
Higher frequency transducers result in which of the
following?
choices: A. Greater sensitivity to slow flow
correct_answer_text: A. Greater sensitivity to slow flow
explanation:
Higher frequency transducers result in decreased
penetration, increased spatial (axial) resolution,
increased energy deposition and a greater
sensitivity in detecting slow flow.

question_text:
Decreasing the pulse repetition frequency results in
which of the following?
choices: C. Increased sensitivity to slow flow
correct_answer_text: C. Increased sensitivity to slow flow
explanation:
Decreasing pulsed repetition frequency results in
increased aliasing, increased sensitivity to slow flow
and images with greater depth.

question_text:
Which of the following is an advantage of using
ultrasound over CT?
explanation:
Ultrasound has better resolution than CT.
Ultrasounds spatial frequency is approximately 2
Ip/mm while CT is 1.5 Ip/mm.

question_text:
If the ultrasound beam frequency increases, how will
the near zone be affected?
explanation:
If the frequency increases, the near field (also
called the Fresnel zone) will increase. Per the wave
equation, an increase in frequency must result in a
decreased wavelength since the speed of sound
stays constant. The equation to calculate the near
field is: Near field = (element size)2/wavelength.
Since the wavelength is in the denominator, a
decrease in the wavelength means an increase in
the near field. Note that the near field is where
ultrasound imaging is typically performed.
Lengthening the near field can improve the quality
and efficiency of imaging.

question_text:
What is the purpose of the backing material in an
ultrasound probe?
explanation:
The backing material, also called the damping
block, REDUCES the pulse length and
INCREASES the bandwidth. The damping block
stops the crystals from vibrating and sending out
sound waves. The reduced pulse length allows for
improved axial resolution.

question_text: What is the typical ultrasound pulse length?
explanation:
A pulse in ultrasound is typically only 2-3
wavelengths long. Short pulses are made possible
by the backing material which reduces the
wavelength and increases the bandwidth. The
reduced pulse length allows for improved axial
resolution.

question_text:
Which mode of ultrasound deposits the most power
within the patient?
explanation:
Pulsed Doppler deposits the most power and B
mode deposits the least power. Bmode = 10-20
mW/cm2; Mmode = 4 x Bmode; Pulsed Doppler =
20 x Bmode. Because Doppler ultrasound has
higher energy (higher thermal and mechanical
indexes), it should not be used during the first
trimester pregnancy.

question_text:
The tissue attenuation of an ultrasound beam by a
material is 5 dB/cm. What percentage of the
ultrasound beam remains at 6 cm depth?
explanation:
The tissue attenuation of the material is 5 dB/cm.
After 6 cm, the beam will be attenuated by 30 dB.
After 10 dB of attenuation, 1/10th of the beam
remains, after 20 dB of attenuation 1/100th of the
beam remains, and after 30 dB of attenuation only
1/1000th of the beam remains. 1/1000th = 0.1%.

question_text:
How is depth of penetration affected by 1) increased
gain and 2) increased power in ultrasound?
choices: A. Increased gain or power = unchanged penetration depth
B. Increased gain = unchanged penetration depth; increased power = increased penetration depth
C. Increased gain = increased penetration depth; increased power = unchanged penetration depth
D. Increased gain or power = increased penetration depth
correct_answer_text: B. Increased gain = unchanged penetration depth; increased power = increased penetration depth
explanation:
Changing the gain ONLY affects the brightness of
the image on the monitor. It does NOT change the
function of the ultrasound transducer and does NOT
affect the depth of penetration. In contrast,
increasing the power will increase the depth of
penetration.

question_text:
How does the velocity of the ultrasound pulse
change with the pulse frequency?
explanation:
The velocity that a pulse travels is independent of
the pulse frequency. However, the velocity of the
pulse will change depending on the tissue type. For
instance, pulses travel slower through fat (1460
m/s) than soft tissues (1540 m/s). Since the
ultrasound machine assumes a constant speed of
sound in human tissues of 1540 m/sec, collections
of fat can result in misplacement of echo signal,
known as speed displacement artifact.

question_text:
Ultrasound axial resolution depends on which of the
following?
explanation:
Axial resolution = spatial pulse length/2. As the
spatial pulse length decrease, the axial resolution
improves. Since pulse lengths are fairly constant at
2-3 wavelengths, the most obvious way to decrease
the spatial pulse length is to shorten the wavelength
(i.e., increase the pulse frequency). By increasing
the pulse frequency you shorten the spatial pulse
length and increase the axial resolution. This is a
central principle of ultrasound: higher frequency
probes have great axial resolution but poor
penetration and lower frequency probes have poor
axial resolution but great penetration. Higher
frequency transducers also result in increased
energy deposition and a greater sensitivity in
detecting slow flow.

question_text:
The mechanical index of an ultrasound is most
important in which setting?
choices: A. Superficial imaging with high frequency probes
B. Use of intravenous ultrasound contrast
correct_answer_text: B. Use of intravenous ultrasound contrast
explanation:
Mechanical index is MOST important in the setting
of a contrast-enhanced ultrasound examination.
Ultrasound contrasts are based on microbubbles. If
the mechanical index is too high it can cause the
bubbles to rupture potentially resulting in capillary
damage.

question_text:
What is the purpose of the matching layer in the
ultrasound probe?
explanation:
The purpose of the matching layer is to reduce
acoustic reflection. Without a matching layer, the
echo would pass directly from the PZT crystal to the
skin. The acoustic impedance of PZT crystals is
vastly greater than the acoustic impedance of soft
tissue. Because of this, a PZT-skin interface would
result in a large amount of the echo beam being
reflected — severely degrading image quality. The
matching layer has an acoustic impedance value
that is in between PZT and soft tissue. This
eliminates the PZT-soft tissue interface and
decreases the reflective intensity.

question_text:
Which dimension is likely to have the best resolution
in ultrasound?
explanation:
Axial resolution tends to be best in ultrasound. Axial
resolution tends to be 4 times better than lateral
resolution. Therefore, if the axial resolution is 1mm,
then the lateral resolution is likely to be around
4mm. Elevational resolution is variable and is
primarily dependent on slice thickness.

question_text:
Increasing which of the following settings on an
ultrasound machine will increase patient exposure
and artifact generation?
explanation:
Increasing power will increase patient exposure and
artifact generation. Changing the gain only changes
the brightness of the image on the monitor and
does not alter the ultrasound output.

question_text:
How will doubling the element size affect size of the
near field (N) in ultrasound imaging?
explanation:
Near field = (element size)42 / wavelength.
Therefore, increases in the element size will
increase the near field (also called the Fresnel
zone) by a square of the increase. So doubling the
element size means that the near field will be
increased by a factor of 4. Exploiting this concept, 5
elements in an array are often pulsed
simultaneously, effectively creating an element that
is 5 times wider. This lengthens the near zone by a
factor of 25. Since the wavelength is in the
denominator, a decrease in the wavelength (or
increase in frequency) means an increase in the
near field. Note that the near field is where
ultrasound imaging is typically performed.
Lengthening the near field can improve the quality
and efficiency of imaging.

question_text:
Which interface is likely to have the highest reflected
intensity in ultrasound?
explanation:
Reflected intensity indicates the portion of the echo
that is reflected at a tissue interface. The likelihood
of reflection is greater for interfaces of tissue with
significantly different acoustic impedances. The
lung diaphragm interface is essentially a soft
tissue/air interface: aerated lung has a high
acoustic impedance similar to that of air and
diaphragm has a lower acoustic impedance similar
to soft tissue. This large disparity in acoustic
impedances results in a high reflected intensity with
over 50% of the echo being reflected. The other
interfaces are soft tissue/soft tissue (skin/muscle
and kidney/fat) or soft tissue/fluid (stomach
wall/fluid), which are much closer in their acoustic
impedance values. The reflective intensities for
these interfaces are <1%.

question_text:
Increasing the dynamic range in ultrasound will have
which of the following effects on image contrast?
explanation:
Increasing the dynamic range reduces the

contrast. To understand this, think of the dynamic
range as a window width. If you widen the window
on a Chest CT, the contrast suffers. If you use a
very narrow window, the contrast is improved. In the
same way, increasing the dynamic range will
decrease ultrasound contrast, and decreasing the
dynamic range will increase the contrast.

question_text:
What are the typical imaging depths for a 3 MHz and
a 10 MHz ultrasound transducer?
explanation:
Memorize these numbers. The typical imaging
depth for a 3 MHz probe is 20 cm and the typical
depth for a 10 MHz probe is 6 cm. Pulse
attenuation decreases with pulse frequency. This is
why lower frequency probes have greater
penetration (i.e. a greater imaging depth).

question_text: Which is least likely to affect lateral resolution?
explanation:
Lateral resolution can be improved by decreasing
the beam width, increasing focusing, or increasing
the line density. To resolve two adjacent objects in
the lateral dimension, the objects must be
separated into different beams. The narrower the
beam width, the more likely the object will fall into
separate beams. Therefore, all factors that affect
resolution do so by manipulating beam width. For
instance, increased focusing can result in a narrow
beam width and increased lateral resolution.
Likewise, increasing the line density means
narrowing the elements size — a narrow element
size creates a narrow beam. Spatial pulse length
will NOT affect lateral resolution; Axial resolution =
spatial pulse length/2.

question_text:
Which of the following is independent of Doppler
angle?
explanation:
Only power Doppler is independent of Doppler
angle. Power Doppler is more sensitive in detecting
slow flow and can be of value in differentiating
thrombus from slow flow. Power Doppler cannot be
used to determine flow direction or velocity. Aliasing
is seen with color Doppler. Flash artifact is seen
with power Doppler because it is more sensitive to
transducer motion.

question_text:
The following information can be obtained from
spectral Doppler evaluation except:
choices: C. Type of flow (normal versus abnormal for a given vessel)
explanation:
Depth of the vessel is not determined by spectral
Doppler. The direction flow can be determined by
the spectral above or below the baseline. Spectral
Doppler produces the whole spectrum flow
velocities in the interrogation window. Vessels
demonstrate specific spectral waveforms (for
example, low resistance, high resistance). Deviation
from the normal pattern signifies abnormalities like
stenosis etc.

question_text:
Doppler shift is dependent on all of the following
except:
explanation:
Transducer thickness is not related to the Doppler

shift. Doppler shift = 2 x Ft x cos(Q) x V/c where Ft
is transmitted frequency, V is velocity of blood flow,
c is speed of sound in tissue and Q is the Doppler

angle.

question_text:
Which one of the following is the main reason for
application of gel to skin surface for ultrasound
imaging?
choices: A. It increases the frequency of transmitted echoes
B. It displaces air, which reduces reflection and improves image quality
C. It reduces friction and decreases scan duration
D. It prevents heat production and decreases patient discomfort
correct_answer_text: B. It displaces air, which reduces reflection and improves image quality
explanation:
Gel is applied to skin for imaging to displace air and
reduce reflection from skin surface. Gel reduces
friction but is not the primary reason for its
application. Gel does not present heat generation in
the tissues. Frequency of transmitted echoes is
dependent only on the frequency of transducer
used and is not altered by gel application.

question_text: Which of the following is a diffuse reflector?
explanation:
In general, smooth large surfaces are specular
reflectors, for example, bladder, spleen, diaphragm
and long bones. Small irregular surfaces like RBCs
are diffuse reflectors.

question_text:
Which of the following interactions of ultrasound with
tissues is used to form gray-scale images?
explanation:
Reflection of ultrasound waves is responsible for
formation of images. Absorption and refraction
result in attenuation of sound and decrease in
echoes available for image formation. Doppler shift
is not involved in formation of gray scale images.

question_text:
In ultrasound imaging which of the following is the
most likely result of switching to a higher frequency
transducer?
choices: B. increased speed of sound in soft tissue
D. increased maximum depth of penetration
E. longer ultrasound wavelength in tissue
explanation:
The ultrasound attenuation coefficient increases
with frequency. Therefore, by switching to a higher
frequency transducer, one would increase
ultrasound attenuation but better spatial (axial)
resolution. This is a central principle of ultrasound:
higher frequency probes have great axial resolution
but poor penetration and lower frequency probes
have poor axial resolution but great penetration.
Higher frequency transducers also result in
increased energy deposition and a greater
sensitivity in detecting slow flow.

question_text:
What is the most likely benefit for ultrasound spatial
compound imaging compared with the conventional
B-mode imaging?
choices: C. Reduced inherent artifacts such as speckles and clutter
D. Increased maximum depth of penetration
correct_answer_text: C. Reduced inherent artifacts such as speckles and clutter
explanation:
One shortcoming of the conventional ultrasound
imaging is that it is subject to “speckles” (caused by
constructive and destructive interactions of sound
waves generated from tissue) and “clutter” (a
generic name for ultrasound artifacts caused by
side lobes, grating lobes, reverberations and so on).
Ultrasound spatial compound imaging combines
multiple frames of images acquired at various
frequencies or with the ultrasound beam steered at
various angles and then forms the multiple frames
into one. As the most likely benefit, inherent
artifacts such as speckles and clutter are reduced.

question_text:
Compared with a conventional linear array
transducer, a 1.5D linear array ultrasound transducer
provides:
explanation:
With a conventional array transducer, the array of
elements is aligned in a single row. Therefore, the
elevational resolution is fixed, converging to a
certain point followed by divergence in the far field.
However, with a 1.5D array transducer, its
elevational resolution (i.e. slice thickness) can be
adjusted electronically as well, just like the focal
zones in the lateral direction. This improves the
elevational resolution, especially in the regions
close to the transducer surface where conventional
array transducers have degraded resolution
because of slice thickness that is hard to focus.

question_text:
In real time B-mode ultrasound, lateral resolution
improves with increased:
explanation:
Higher scan-line density generates better lateral
spatial resolution. On the other hand, when more
scan lines are used to form the cross-sectional
image, the frame rate must be reduced.

question_text: In ultrasound, an M-mode display presents:
choices: B. Moving anatomy as a function of time
C. An extended field of view for a B-mode image
D. Blood flow in spectral format
correct_answer_text: B. Moving anatomy as a function of time
explanation:
"M" in M-mode stands for motion. In M-mode, the
echo signal amplitudes are modulated to brightness
levels and displayed as a line with varying shades
of gray. The ultrasound beam line is not swept
across a plane of interest. Instead, the beam is
fixed at one position but observed over scanning
time in order to graph the motion of moving
structures. Therefore, for a stationary structure, a
straight trace is seen; for a moving structure (such
as a heart valve), wavy patterns are generated. M-
mode is used to measure fetal heart rate in first
trimester US because the time-averaged acoustic
intensity delivered to the fetus is lower with M-mode
than with spectral Doppler.

question_text: What is a feature of spoiled gradient echo?
explanation:
Spoiled gradient echo imaging uses shorter
repetition time (TR) because they use a spoiler
gradient after each echo which destroys transverse
magnetization. As a result, images can be obtained
during a single breath hold.

question_text:
Which signal is suppressed in short tau inversion
recovery (STIR) sequences?
explanation:
STIR sequences are obtained when the inversion
time (TI) is chosen to suppress fat. Fluid Attenuated
Inversion Recovery (FLAIR) sequences are
obtained when the TI is chosen to suppress fluid.

question_text:
What MRI sequence is most sensitive for detecting an
infiltrative marrow process?
choices: B. T1-weighted sequence without fat saturation
D. T2-weighted sequence without fat saturation
correct_answer_text: B. T1-weighted sequence without fat saturation
explanation:
Normal marrow fat is hyperintense on T1. Any
infiltrative process will appear hypointense.

question_text:
Which of the following is seen with positive MR
contrast agents?
explanation:
Positive MR contrast agents, e.g. gadolinium = T1
hyperintensity = T1 shortening. T2 hyperintensity =
T2 prolongation.

question_text:
An atom exhibiting nuclear magnetic resonance must
have which of the following?
explanation:
In order for an atom to exhibit nuclear magnetic
resonance, it must have an odd number of protons
and/or neutrons. This is why calcium-40 (20
neutrons and 20 protons) appears "black" on MR.
Although there are other atoms in the human body
besides hydrogen (1 proton) that have an odd
number of neutrons or protons, hydrogen is the
most abundant and therefore contributes the most
to the signal.

question_text:
For a given RF pulse, increasing the gradient in the z-
axis results in which of the following?
explanation:
Increasing the gradient in the z-axis results in
thinner slices.

question_text: Increasing slice thickness results in:
explanation:
Increasing the size of a voxel results in increased
signal per voxel, and therefore increased signal to
noise (noise is constant). Increasing the size of a
voxel in the z-axis results in decreased spatial
resolution in the z-axis and increased partial volume
averaging.

question_text:
Hydrogen atoms in fat and water are in-phase at what
time points in a 1.5 Tesla magnet?
explanation: In-phase and out-of-phase conditions occur twice per cycle, or approximately every 2.2 msec at 1.5T. After an excitation pulse on a 1.5T magnet, hydrogen atoms in water and fat are in phase at 0, 4.4, 8.8, 13.2 ms, etc. Water and fat are out of phase at 2.2, 6.6, 11.0 ms, etc. At 3T, phase cycling is twice as fast: water and fat are in phase at 0, 2.2, 4.4, 6.6 ms, etc., and out of phase at 1.1, 3.3, 5.5 ms, etc.

question_text:
What is the signal intensity of flowing blood on
conventional gradient echo sequence?
explanation:
On conventional gradient echo sequence, flowing
blood is a hyperintense and therefore a bright blood
technique. This is because gradient echo pulses
are not slice selective. Attenuation refers to
computed tomography.

question_text:
What is the signal intensity of flowing blood on
conventional spin echo T1 sequence?
explanation:
On conventional spin echo sequences (T1 & T2),
flowing blood appears hypointense (flow void or
high velocity signal loss) and therefore SE
sequences are black blood techniques. This is
because blood in the slice at the time of the 180
refocusing pulse did not receive the initial 90
excitation pulse (the blood that did receive the 90
degree excitation pulse is long gone by the time the
180 refocusing pulse is applied). Attenuation refers
to computed tomography.

question_text:
Blood oxygen level determination (BOLD) techniques
measure differences in?
explanation:
BOLD is a functional MRI (fMRI) technique which
measures subtle differences in local magnetic fields
created by differences in oxyhemoglobin which in
turn are created by vessel autoregulation. Images
are obtained at rest and during a task (finger tap) to
determine the eloquent cortex needed to perform
the task. During the task vessels dilate to supply
more oxyhemoglobin to the metabolically active
regions needed to perform the task
(autoregulation). This results in delivery of more
oxyhemoglobin which in turn create larger
differences in field inhomogeneities (T2*).

question_text:
When compared to conventional spin echo imaging,
gradient recalled echo imaging results in?
explanation:
Gradient recalled echo imaging (GRE) results in
shorter TR and therefore shorter image acquisition
times. However, it is more sensitive to magnetic
field inhomogeneities and magnetic susceptibility
and results in decreased signal to noise ratios.
Being more sensitive to magnetic susceptibility can
be advantageous, however, as is the case when
accessing for hemorrhage. It is a disadvantage
when imaging metallic devices like joint prosthesis.

question_text:
If you want to determine if a T1 hypointense mass is
a cyst, what additional sequence would be helpful?
explanation:
You want to perform a T2 sequence to see if this
mass is of fluid signal intensity (T2 hyperintense).
T2 sequences use Long TR (22000 ms) and Long
TE (= 70 ms). T1 sequence = Short TR (s 500 ms)
and Short TE (<20 ms). PD sequence = Long TR
(2000-3000 ms) and Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text:
Which of the following has the longest T2 relaxation
time?
explanation:
T2 relaxation time (spin-spin relaxation time) is a
tissue property. The longer the T2 relaxation time
the more hyperintense that tissue is on T2 weighted
images. CSF is the brightest on T2 weighted
images and therefore has the longest T2 relaxation
time followed by white matter, adipose tissue and
then muscle.

question_text:
If the MRI technologist chooses a short TR, what
sequence weighting will the images acquire?
explanation:
Short TR (repetition times) are chosen to bring out
differences in tissue T1 relaxation times. In other
words, the greatest difference in signal attributable
to T1 relaxation times occurs with shorter repetition
times. T1 sequence = Short TR (s 500 ms) and
Short TE (<20 ms). T2 = Long TR (22000 ms) and
Long TE (= 70 ms). PD sequence = Long TR (2000-
3000 ms) and Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text:
Most of the noise created by MRI is secondary to
activation of which of the following?
explanation:
The loud "clicks" heard during an MR scan are
secondary to activation of the gradient coils and
their interaction with the main magnet field.
Because the noise is so loud, patients must wear
both ear plugs and ear phones during their scan.

question_text:
Which of the following demonstrates elevated lipid
and lactate peaks and a diminished NAA peak ona
brain magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS)
study?
explanation:
Elevated lipid is seen with cell membrane
destruction. Elevated lactate is seen with anaerobic
metabolism. NAA is created by neurons so
decreased NAA indicates fewer neurons. An
abscess demonstrates these characteristics.

question_text:
The MR signal is a measure of the magnetic field
strength oriented in which direction?
choices: A. Antiparallel to the main magnetic field
B. Perpendicular to the main magnetic field
C. Diagonal to the main magnetic field
D. Parallel to the main magnetic field
correct_answer_text: B. Perpendicular to the main magnetic field
explanation:
The actual MR signal is the amplitude of the
magnetic field created perpendicular to the applied
magnetic field (transverse magnetization vector).

question_text:
Bright blood MRA techniques use which of the
following?

In conventional spin echo imaging, which sequence
takes the longest to acquire?
explanation:
T2 sequences use a long TE and long TRandasa
result T2 weighted images take the longest to
acquire.

question_text:
Decay of synchronous precession is known as which
of the following?
explanation:
After a 90 degree radiofrequency pulse is applied,
precessing protons of the same frequency will begin
to precess synchronously. When the RF pulse is
turned off, the protons will begin to relax and
precess asynchronously/randomly. This rate of
relaxation is tissue dependent and known as T2
relaxation.

question_text:
Instead of a head coil, a body coil is used to image
the brain. How is the image quality affected?
choices: A. Decreased signal and decreased noise
B. Decreased signal and increased noise
C. Increased signal and increased noise
D. Increased signal and decreased noise
correct_answer_text: B. Decreased signal and increased noise
explanation:
The further the RF coil is from the body part of
interest, the smaller the signal, greater the noise
and smaller the signal to noise ratio. Alternatively,
the closer the coil is to the body part of interest the
greater the signal, smaller the noise and greater the
signal to noise ratio. This is why there are specific
coils for different body parts such as head coils,
body coils and extremity coils. This is also why the
MR bore is smaller than that of the a CT.
Unfortunately, smaller bore size often isn't tolerated
by patients with claustrophobia.

question_text:
The T1 relaxation time is shortest for which of the
following?
explanation:
T1 relaxation time is a tissue property. The shorter
the T1 relaxation time, the more hyperintense that
tissue is on T1 weighted images. Adipose tissue is
the brightest on T1 weighted images and, therefore,
has the shortest T1 relaxation time followed by
white matter, muscle and CSF.

question_text:
What will shimming do to magnetic field
inhomogeneities?
explanation:
The goal of the MRI magnet is to produce a uniform
magnetic field. Unfortunately, no magnet is perfect
and as a result there are inhomogeneities in the
static magnet field. Innomogeneities result in
artifacts. Shimming is the process of minimizing
these inhomogeneities in an effort to create a more
uniform magnetic field.

question_text:
What will occur as the strength of the external
magnetic field is increased?
choices: A. Signal to noise increases proportionately
C. Signal to noise increases exponentially
correct_answer_text: A. Signal to noise increases proportionately
explanation:
Increasing the strength of the external magnetic
field (i.e. from 1 Tesla to 3 Tesla) results in a
proportional increase in signal and signal to noise
ratio.

question_text:
When a 90 degrees RF pulse at the Larmor frequency
is applied to protons in a static magnetic field, what
is the net magnetization in the direction of the
magnetic field?
explanation:
When a 90 degree radiofrequency pulse at the
Larmor frequency is applied to protons within a
static magnetic field, the result is a net
magnetization of 0 in the direction of the magnetic
field (Mz = 0). A 90 degree pulse also caused the
protons to precess in a synchronous manner.

question_text:
Which of the following would differentiate pulsation
artifact from a liver lesion on an MR?
choices: B. Swap the phase and frequency encoding directions
correct_answer_text: B. Swap the phase and frequency encoding directions
explanation:
If it is pulsation artifact, then swapping the
frequency and phase encoding directions will
change the position of the artifact, i.e. pulsation
artifact is only seen in the phase encoding direction.

question_text:
Aneurysm clips are a contraindication to MRI unless
it is which of the following?
explanation:
If there is documented proof that the aneurysm clip
is "MR safe" or "MR conditional," the patient may
have an MR scan. Also, if there is documentation
that the clip is made of titanium, the patient may
have an MR scan.

question_text:
If a static magnetic field is applied, antiparallel
protons will align parallel with magnetic field
according to which of the following tissue
characteristics?
explanation:
When a static magnetic field is applied individual
proton, dipoles line up in either a parallel or
antiparallel direction. Because the parallel direction
is of lower energy, the antiparallel protons begin to
relax into the parallel direction. This rate of
relaxation is tissue dependent and known as T1
relaxation.

question_text:
How is T1 relaxation affected by increasing magnetic
field strength?
explanation:
When a static magnetic field is applied individual
proton, dipoles line up in either a parallel or
antiparallel direction. Because the parallel direction
is of lower energy, the antiparallel protons begin to
relax into the parallel direction. This rate of
relaxation is tissue dependent and known as T1
relaxation. In general, T1 relaxation times increase
with increasing magnetic field strength. At
increasing magnetic field strengths, there are fewer
antiparallel protons available to relax (transfer
energy to the lattice), and therefore the T1 time is
lengthened. In order to achieve the higher signal-to-
noise from stronger magnetic field, longer imaging
time is needed(i.e., due to longer T1 times). T2
times are generally independent of magnetic field
strength.

question_text:
How does MR signal-to-noise change if the sensitive
volume of the radiofrequency (RF) coil is increased?
explanation:
As the sensitive volume of the RF coil increases,
the volume within the coil contributing to image
noise increases. Because of this, large coils have
lower SNR. Conversely, SNR will improve if local
coils and surface coils are used. The further the RF
coil is from the body part of interest, the smaller the
signal, greater the noise and smaller the signal to
noise ratio. Alternatively, the closer the coil is to the
body part of interest the greater the signal, smaller
the noise and greater the signal to noise ratio. This
is why there are specific coils for different body
parts such as head coils, body coils and extremity
coils. This is also why the MR bore is smaller than
that of the a CT. Unfortunately, smaller bore size
often isn't tolerated by patients with claustrophobia.

question_text:
How do ADC (Apparent Diffusion Coefficient) images
can increase the specificity of stroke imaging?
choices: A. Removing susceptibility artifact related to microhemorrhages
B. Differentiating acute infarct from T2 shine-through
D. Better defining the salvageable ischemic penumbra
correct_answer_text: B. Differentiating acute infarct from T2 shine-through
explanation:
Increased signal on diffusion weighted imaging
(DWI) can either represent an acute infarct or T2
shine-through. ADC images are helpful for
differentiating T2 shine-through from acute infarct.

question_text:
In spin echo imaging, which gradient is changed with
every repetition?
explanation:
The phase-encoding gradient is stepped (or
changed) with each repetition (TR).

question_text:
How does fast spin echo (FSE) imaging achieve
faster image acquisition times?
choices: A. By only partially filling K-space
B. By reading out multiple echoes per TR
C. Using a stronger magnetic field
D. By decreasing the TR by 1/3
correct_answer_text: B. By reading out multiple echoes per TR
explanation:
In FSE imaging, there is still only one 90° RF pulse
per TR, but unlike spin echo imaging, FSE imaging
has many 180° excitations per TR. This means that
multiple echoes can be read out for a single TR,
allowing multiple lines of K-space to be filled in a
single TR. Because more information is being
gathered during a single TR, the TR time may need
to be lengthened. But since more information is
being gathered during each TR, there are still huge
time savings with FSE. The number of echoes that
are read out for each TR is known as the echo train
length. An FSE acquisition with an echo train length
of 16 is about 16 times faster than a traditional spin
echo exam. This is because 16 lines of K-space are
being filled per TR, instead of just one.

question_text:
If the receiver bandwidth is increased, what is the
effect on TE in MR?
explanation:
If bandwidth increases, then SNR, mismapping
artifacts (chemical shift artifact and/or
susceptibility), and TE all decrease. The SNR
decreases because increasing the bandwidth
results in increased noise. TE decreases because a
broader bandwidth has a higher amplitude readout
gradient which allows for faster sampling of the MR
signal.

question_text: What is the purpose of shimming in MRI?
choices: B. Correct for patient motion artifact
explanation:
Shimming improves field homogeneity by correcting
imbalances in the main magnetic field. There are
two types of shimming: passive shimming (e.g.,
varying the amount of steel at the magnets poles)
and active shimming (e.g., use of shim coils). A
combination of these two strategies is typically
used. Active shimming with shim coils allows for
finer corrections of the magnetic field. Shimming is
useful in fat suppression imaging and is particularly
important in breast MRI. Partial K-space sampling
can reduce image acquisition time, but does so at
the expense of signal-to-noise ratio. A Faraday
cage is built into walls of an MRI suite to prevent
extraneous radiofrequency interference from
degrading images (i.e., zipper artifact). Radial k-
space filling can reduce the conspicuity of patient
motion artifact.

question_text:
How does gadolinium increase intravascular contrast
in MRI?
choices: A. By increasing the T2 relaxation time of water
B. By decreasing the T2 relaxation time of water
C. By increasing the T1 relaxation time of water
D. By decreasing the T1 relaxation time of water
correct_answer_text: D. By decreasing the T1 relaxation time of water
explanation:
Gadolinium produces increased intravascular
contrast by decreasing the T1 relaxation time of
water. On T1 weighted images, the shorter T1
values produce brighter signal.

question_text:
As spin lattice interactions decrease, what happens
to the T1 relaxation time?
explanation:
As spin-lattice interactions decrease, the T1
relaxation time will increase. Imagine spin-lattice
interactions as transfers of energy that facilitate
recovery of longitudinal magnetization. Therefore,
after an RF pulse flips the magnetization away from
the longitudinal axis, spin-lattice interactions give
you the kick (i.e., energy transfer) needed to
recover the longitudinal magnetization. Therefore, it
there are lots of spin-lattice interactions (i.e., lots of
kicking), then the longitudinal magnetization is
recovered quickly and the T1 is short (e.g., fat). On
the other hand, if there are minimal spin lattice
interactions (i.e., no kicking is occurring), then it will
take a long time to recover longitudinal
magnetization and the T1 will be long (e.g. CSF ).

question_text:
MR spatial resolution tends to be poorest in which
dimension?
explanation:
The Z dimension of the voxel is determined by the
slice thickness. Since the slice thickness is typically
the largest dimension of the voxel, the spatial
resolution will be poorest in this axis.

question_text:
In Gradient Recall Echo (GRE) Imaging, how will
increasing the flip angle affect the characteristics of
T1-weighted imaging?
explanation:
GRE imaging is dependent on 1) the flip angle AND
2) the TE. For TE and TR values compatible with T1
weighting, increasing the flip angle will increase the
T1 weighting. The flip angles used in GRE imaging
tend to be significantly lower that 90-degree flip
angle used in spin echo imaging. Increasing the TE
in GRE imaging will result in greater T2* weighting
(just as increasing the TE in spin echo imaging
results in greater T2 weighting).

question_text:
A long TE (>70 ms) is most likely to result in which of
the following?
explanation:
Relate TE with T2 and TR with T1. Short TR = T1
weighting. Long TR = No T1 weighting. Long TE =
T2 weighting. Short TE = No T2 weighting. T1
sequence = Short TR (s 500 ms) and Short TE (<20
ms). T2 = Long TR (22000 ms) and Long TE (2 70
ms). PD sequence = Long TR (2000-3000 ms) and
Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text:
If the x, y, and z dimensions of the voxel are doubled,
how will the SNR of the MRI image be affected?
choices: A. Decrease by a factor of 2
B. Increase by a factor of 2
C. Decrease by a factor of 8
E. Increase by a factor of 8
correct_answer_text: E. Increase by a factor of 8
explanation:
Signal intensity is directly proportional to voxel
volume. Therefore, doubling each dimension of the
voxel will increase the voxel volume by 243 = 8.
Noise, on the other hand, is independent of the
voxel volume and will not change. Note that this is
an important difference between CT and MRI. In
CT, if you made similar changes to the voxel size,
noise would overall decrease by a factor of the
square root of 8. Since the signal increases by a
factor of 8 and the noise does not change, the SNR
will also increase by a factor of 8.

question_text:
The Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons in a 1
Tesla (T) magnet is 42 MHz. What is the Larmor
frequency of hydrogen protons in a 3T magnet?
explanation:
The rate at which hydrogen protons precess is
DIRECTLY proportional to the magnetic field
strength. Therefore, if the magnetic field strength
triples, the Larmor frequency will triple (42 MHz per
Tesla x 3 Tesla = 126 MHz). Important number to
remember: Larmor frequency for hydrogen protons
is 42 MHz/Tesla.

question_text:
On a 3T scanner, what approximate frequency should
be chosen for the radiofrequency pulses?
choices: D. Any pulse will do, but higher frequencies will deposit more energy
explanation:
To effect (aka, tilt) the magnetic moment of
hydrogen protons, the radiofrequency pulse must
equal the Larmor frequency of the protons. The
Larmor frequency of hydrogen protons in a 1 Tesla
(T) magnet is 42 MHz. Since the Larmor frequency
increases DIRECTLY proportional to the magnetic
field strength, the Larmor frequency will be 3 x 42
MHz = 126 MHz at 3T. So the radiofrequency pulse
selected should be 126 MHz.

question_text:
In spin echo imaging, when is the slice selection
gradient applied?
explanation:
In spin echo imaging, the slice selection gradient
must be applied with every radiofrequency pulse.
Therefore, at a minimum it will be applied during the
90 degree RF pulse at time t= 0 and the 180 degree
pulse at time TE/2. Note that for all subsequent
sequences, TR represents time t=0.

question_text:
The matrix size is 64 x 128. How long will it take to
acquire the MR image in spin echo?
explanation: The time required to acquire an image is the number of phase encoding steps multiplied by the TR. To optimize imaging time, the smaller matrix dimension should be the number of phase encoding steps and the larger matrix dimension should be the number of frequency encoding steps. Thus, the image acquisition time is 64 x TR.

question_text:
Which tissue will have the shortest inversion time
(Tl)?
explanation:
Inversion recovery imaging is used to null the signal
from certain tissue types. To achieve this, you start
with a 180 degree pulse. Next you let the tissues
start to recover their longitudinal magnetization. The
inversion time is the time that it takes for the tissue
of interest to recover to 90 degrees. The question is
simply asking which tissue recovers its longitudinal
magnetization the fastest? In T1-weighted imaging,
fat is bright, grey matter is grey, and CSF is black.
This means that fat recovers the fastest, grey
matter recovers at an intermediate rate, and CSF
recovers the slowest. Therefore, fat has the shortest
TI (~150 ms) and CSF has the longest TI (2,400
ms). Note that STIR, a fat suppression inversion
recovery sequence, stands for “SHORT tau
inversion recovery.” FLAIR, a fluid suppression
sequence, stands for “Fluid attenuated inversion
recovery.”

question_text:
Signal loss due to T2* decay is caused by which type
of 1) spin-spin interactions and 2) magnetic field
inhomogeneities.
explanation:
T2* decay describes the loss of net transverse
magnetization due to dephasing. There are 2
contributors to T2* decay: 1) random spin-spin
interactions and 2) non-random local magnetic field
inhomogeneities (e.g., such as those caused by
paramagnetic materials). Since magnetic field
inhomogeneities are non-random, signal loss
related to field inhomogeneities can be recovered
with a 180 degree refocusing pulse. In contrast, the
random nature of spin-spin interactions means that
they cannot be recovered with a 180 degree
refocusing pulse. Note that spin echo imaging uses
a 180 degree refocusing pulse to recover signal
decay related to local field inhomogeneities. In
contrast, gradient recall echo imaging does not
contain a 180 degree refocusing pulse. This is why
gradient recall echo imaging is prone to artifacts
related to local field inhomogeneities (e.g.,
susceptibility artifact).

question_text:
Increasing from a 1.5T to a 3T magnet will most likely
do what to the SNR?
choices: B. Increase SNR by a factor of 4
C. Increase SNR by a factor of 2
D. Decrease SNR by a factor of 4
E. Decrease SNR by a factor of 2
correct_answer_text: C. Increase SNR by a factor of 2
explanation:
Increasing the magnetic field strength from 1.5T to
3.0T increases the SNR by a factor of 2. Signal
intensity increases approximately linearly with
magnetic field strength. Noise is essentially
unchanged. Therefore, SNR will increase
approximately linearly with magnetic field strength.
So if the magnetic field strength doubles, SNR will
essentially double.

question_text: SNR is increased by all of the following except:
choices: B. Signal averages (i.e., increasing the number of acquisitions)
explanation:
SNR is increased by increasing FOV, slice
thickness, signal averages, and field strength. In
contrast, as the imaging matrix increases, the SNR
decreases and the resolution increases.

question_text:
If the matrix size and slice thickness are held
constant, how does decreasing the FOV affect MR
spatial resolution?
explanation:
If the matrix size and slice thickness are held
constant, decreasing the FOV will result in higher
spatial resolution. The improved resolution is
achieved by decreasing the voxel size. Note,
however, that a smaller FOV will increase the
likelihood of aliasing/wraparound artifact.

question_text:
How does partial K-space acquisition affect
acquisition time and SNR?
choices: A. Acquisition time increases and SNR decreases
C. Acquisition time decreases and SNR increases
explanation:
Partial K-space acquisition can be used to reduce
imaging acquisition times. But, this time saving
comes at the cost of reduced SNR.

question_text:
If the receiver bandwidth is increased, how is the
SNR affected?
explanation:
If bandwidth increases, then SNR, mismapping
artifacts (e.g., chemical shift artifact), and TE will all
DECREASE. The SNR decreases because
increasing the bandwidth results in increased noise.
TE decreases because a broader bandwidth has a
higher amplitude readout gradient which allows for
faster sampling of the MR signal.

question_text:
Complete recovery of longitudinal magnetization is
achieved after a time period equal to:
explanation:
T1 is a measure of the longitudinal relaxation time.
Recovery of longitudinal magnetization occurs
exponentially with time at a predictable rate: 63%
has recovered by T1, 87% by 2x T1, 95% by 3 x
T1, and essentially all of the longitudinal
magnetization has recovered by 4x T1.

question_text:
The T1 relaxation time can never be the T2
relaxation time.
explanation:
T1 is a measure of the time it takes to recover
longitudinal magnetization. At 4 X T1, the
longitudinal magnetization is completely recovered.
T2, on the other hand, is a measure of the time it
takes for transverse component of magnetization to
dephase. By 4 X T2, complete dephasing has
occurred. If longitudinal magnetization has
completely recovered, then no transverse
magnetization remains — phased or dephased.
Taken together, this means that T1 can never be
LESS than T2.

question_text:
In spin echo imaging, when is the frequency
encoding gradient is applied at?
explanation:
The frequency-encoding gradient is applied while
the echo is being read out. Since the echo is
collected at time TE, the frequency-encoding
gradient should be applied at time TE.

question_text:
Per FDA regulations, what is the maximum specific
absorption rate (SAR) for MR imaging of the head
lasting over 10 minutes?
explanation:
The specific absorption rate (SAR) is defined as the
average RF energy deposited in the body and
converted into heat per unit mass and time. The
FDA regulates the maximum specific absorption
rate for MRI imaging of the head and body. The
maximum SAR is 3 W/Kg for head imaging over 10
minutes and 4 W/kg for whole-body imaging over
15 minutes.

question_text:
The matrix size is 64 x 128. How many times is the
echo sampled in spin echo imaging?
explanation:
The question is asking for how many frequency
encoding steps. The matrix size = frequency
encoding steps x phase encoding steps. There are
2 options for filling a matrix size of 64 x 128: 1)
During each TR, fill a line of K-space by sampling
the echo 64 times (64 frequency encoding steps).
This would then require 128 repetitions to fill the
remainder of the matrix (128 phase encoding
steps). The time required to acquire the image by
this strategy is TR times the number of phase
encoding steps, or 128 x TR. OR 2) During each
TR, fill a line of K-space by sampling the echo 128
times (128 frequency encoding steps). This would
then require 64 repetitions to fill the remainder of
the matrix (64 phase encoding steps). The time
required to acquire the image by this strategy is 64
x TR. By using the second option, the imaging time
reduced by a half without sacrificing any image
quality. Therefore, the smaller matrix dimension will
MOST LIKELY be the number of phase encoding
steps and the larger matrix dimension will be the
number of frequency encoding steps. So 128 will be
the number of frequency encoding steps, meaning
that the echo is sampled 128 times.

question_text:
What is a primary determinant of noise in MR
imaging?
explanation:
Receiver bandwidth is the range of frequencies
used to sample the echo and is the primary
determinant of noise in MRI. As the bandwidth
increases, the noise increases, and the SNR
deceases. This should not be confused with
transmit bandwidth, which refers to the RF
excitation pulse used for slice selection. A second
determinant of noise is the size of the coil. As the
sensitive volume of an RF coil increases, the
volume of protons contributing to image noise also
increases. Thus, coils with larger sensitive volumes,
such as body coils, tend to have greater noise. The
use of local and surface coils results in reduced
noise.

question_text:
How will increasing the number of excitations (signal
averaging) affect the SNR in MRI?
explanation:
Increasing the number of excitations (known as
signal averaging) is a technique that is used to
improve SNR. The improvement in SNR can be
approximated by taking the square root of the
number of excitations.

question_text:
How does increasing voxel size affect MR signal
intensity and noise?
choices: B. Noise increases, but signal intensity is independent of voxel size
C. Signal intensity increases, but noise is independent of voxel size
D. Signal intensity increases, but noise decreases
E. Both are independent of voxel size
correct_answer_text: C. Signal intensity increases, but noise is independent of voxel size
explanation:
Signal intensity increases, but noise is independent
of voxel size. Signal intensity increases with the
number of excited protons contributing to the echo.
Since increasing the voxel size increases the
number of protons contributing to the echo, it also
increases the signal intensity. There is an important
difference between CT and MRI. In CT, increased
signal = decreased noise (or mottle). This is not true
in MRI. In MRI, noise tends to be independent of
the voxel volume. Instead, noise is a function of
receiver bandwidth and RF coil characteristics.

question_text: Voxel size is dependent on all of the following except:
explanation:
Voxel size is dependent on matrix size, FOV, and
slice thickness EXCEPT field strength. Note that the
voxel size and all of the choices -- magnetic field
strength, matrix size, FOV, and slice thickness -- will
affect SNR.

question_text:
After an initial 60-degree RF pulse, how is magnitude
of the longitudinal magnetization vector (Mz)
compared to magnitude of the transverse
magnetization vector (Mxy)?
explanation:
After a 60 degree RF pulse, Mz < Mxy. Similarly
after a 90 degree RF pulse, Mz = 0 while Mxy at
maximum magnitude.

question_text:
Which molecule cannot be imaged using magnetic
resonance?
explanation:
Calcium-40 is not a candidate for MRI. A nucleus
must have a nuclear magnetic moment to be
visualized on MRI. Nuclei with an odd number of
protons or neutrons have a net nuclear magnetic
moment. In contrast, nuclei with an even number of
protons and neutrons have no net nuclear magnetic
moment. i.e., the individual magnetic moments of
the protons and neutrons cancel each other out for
a net magnetic moment of 0. The question is simply
asking, does the nucleus have an odd atomic mass
or not? All the choices except Calcium have an odd
number of protons or neutrons as manifested by an
odd atomic mass. Therefore, each of their nuclei
contain a net nuclear magnetic moment and these
molecules can be imaged with MRI. Calcium-40, on
the other hand, has an even number of protons and
neutrons. Therefore, the nucleus of Calcium-40 has
no nuclear magnetic moment and it CANNOT be
used for MRI. It is also why bone (mostly Calcium)
produces no MR signal.

question_text:
Time-varying magnetic field gradients are most
associated with which of the following patient-related
complications?
explanation:
During MR procedures, time-varying magnetic field
gradients may result in nerve or muscle stimulation
inducing electrical fields. The likelinood and severity
of peripheral nerve stimulation is dependent on the
rate of change of the magnetic field and the
duration of the radiofrequency. There is a
theoretical potential for harmful cardiac or brain
stimulation, specifically cardiac fibrillation. However,
studies suggest that the risk of cardiac stimulation is
negligible for commonly used MRI field gradients.

question_text:
To avoid potential adverse health affects associated
with MRI, whole-body MRI imaging performed on
adults should not result in an increase in the core
body temperature by more than how many degrees?
explanation:
1 degree Celsius. Note that this threshold is less at
0.5 degrees Celsius for infants.

question_text: Which sequence is effective TE used?
explanation:
In fast/turbo spin echo (FSE/TSE) imaging, simple
TE is replaced by effective echo time (TEeff), the
time at which the central lines of k-space are being
filled.

question_text:
Diffusion tensor magnetic resonance imaging (DTI)
uses how many diffusion gradient directions?
explanation:
DTI collects information from all the diffusion
weighted images (in at least SIX directions,
covering 3D space) and tries to sum up all that
information about where water can "diffuse to" in
each voxel. In white matter, diffusion follows the
"pathway of least resistance" along the white matter
tract; this direction of maximum diffusivity along the
white-matter fibers is projected into the final image.

question_text:
Which of the following is true regarding wireless RF
(Wifi) technology used for patient monitoring?
choices: A. Is prohibited inside the magnet room
B. Can be used inside the magnet room if specifically designed for MRI installations
C. Is not used in MRI facilities
D. Can be used on patients undergoing MRI examinations without restrictions
E. Is required for inpatient MRI procedures in hospital installations
correct_answer_text: B. Can be used inside the magnet room if specifically designed for MRI installations
explanation:
Wifi can be used inside the magnet room if
specifically designed for MRI installations. Wireless
patient monitoring is becoming widespread in
hospitals and other in-patient facilities. Dedicated
WiFi monitoring equipment that uses very high
frequency RF signals (2.4 GHz and above) for data
transfer communications is available for operations
within the MRI magnet room. If installed correctly, it
does not interfere with operations of the MRI
scanner and can be quite useful in monitoring of
patients undergoing MRI examinations.

question_text: What is the artifact in the image due to?
explanation:
Fat saturation techniques rely heavily on field
homogeneity. Poor fat saturation is seen at the
edges of the image where fat saturation failure is
known to occur. In the right image, there is poor fat
saturation at the orbital floors due to bone/air
interface; this could be mistaken for pathologic
enhancement.

question_text:
There is poor fat saturation at the edges of an image.
This artifact is likely due to?
explanation:
Fat saturation techniques rely heavily on field
homogeneity. Field inhomogeneities increase as the
distance from the magnetic isocenter increases.
Therefore, large FOVs are more likely to have poor
fat saturation than smaller FOVs. In other words,
when there is poor fat saturation on an image it will
be most severe at the periphery and will be more
likely to be seen with larger FOVs.

question_text: What is the name of this artifact?
explanation:
This is an example of pulsatile motion from the
femoral artery creating periodic ghosting artifact,
also known as pulsation artifact. Pulsation artifact is
a motion artifact and like all motion artifacts, occurs
in the phase-encoding direction.

question_text: Which direction is the artifact?
explanation:
This is an example of CSF pulsation at the 4th
ventricle creating periodic ghosting artifact, also
known as pulsation artifact. Pulsation artifact is a
motion artifact and like all motion artifacts, occurs in
the phase-encoding direction, horizontally across
the image. Simply remember: only chemical shift
artifact is in the frequency-encoding direction.

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Aliasing artifact (also known as wraparound artifact)
is due to inadequate sampling of the MR signal.
Aliasing occurs when FOV does not include the
entire object. That portion of the object outside the
FOV is wrapped-around onto the image. The artifact
occurs in the phase-encoding direction and can
therefore be used to determine in which direction
phase encoding was used. In 3-D imaging, the
phase encoding direction is both in the Y-axis and
the Z-axis, so aliasing can occur in both. Options for
correcting aliasing artifact include: increasing FOV,
oversampling the data, switching the phase and
frequency encoding direction, or applying pre-
saturation bands to areas outside of FOV.

question_text: What is the artifact shown on CT?
explanation:
Streak artifact is caused by beam hardening and
occurs when imaging highly attenuating material
such as metal (in this case, aneurysm clips) or the
petrous temporal bone. Streak artifact is secondary
to the lower energy x-ray waves of a polyenergetic
beam being preferentially absorbed. Some of the
higher energy x-ray waves that are not absorbed
subsequently pass through the lower attenuating
surrounding tissues unimpeded and are
subsequently erroneously interpreted.

question_text: What is the artifact shown on MR?
explanation:
In this example, susceptibility artifact is due to
aneurysm clips. Susceptibility artifact occurs
secondary to material that creates local field
inhomogeneities like metal, blood products and
calcium. Susceptibility artifact increases at higher
magnetic field strength and TE (in other words: use
1.5T, shorter TE sequences, higher bandwidth
when imaging metallic implants/prosthesis).
Blooming is susceptibility artifact and is used to
describe an increase in size of the artifact on more
heavily T2 weighted sequences like gradient echo
sequences (long TE).

question_text: Which direction is the phase-encoding direction?
choices: D. Both X and Y dimensions
explanation:
Ghosting and smearing artifacts are produced by
patient motion, both voluntary (e.g., patient
movement) and involuntary (e.g., eye motion). It
typically is most apparent in the phase-encoding
direction, which is Y-dimension in this case. Simply
remember: only chemical shift artifact is in the
frequency-encoding direction.

question_text: The artifact in the image occurs in which direction?
choices: A. Anti-parallel to the main field
B. Both phase and frequency encoding directions
D. Parallel to the main field
explanation:
Ghosting and smearing artifacts are produced by
patient motion, both voluntary (e.g., patient
movement) and involuntary (e.g., eye motion). It
typically is most apparent in the phase-encoding
direction. Simply remember: only chemical shift
artifact is in the frequency-encoding direction.

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
The anterior aspects of the globes are obscured
due to susceptibility artifact from heavy metals in
the patient’s mascara. Susceptibility artifact is the
result of microscopic variations in the magnetic field
strength (aka. gradients) that are produced by
substances of differing magnetic susceptibility. The
gradients lead to dephasing of spins and frequency
shifts of the surrounding tissues. The prominence of
susceptibility artifact is intensified on gradient echo
sequences.

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Images obtained from a large patient reveal loss of
signal in the center of the image, consistent with
standing wave or dielectric artifact.

question_text: Which direction is the frequency-encoding direction?
choices: C. Both X and Y dimensions
explanation:
Zipper artifacts are bands of electronic noise
parallel to the phase-encoding and perpendicular to
the frequency-encoding directions. They're due to
interference from outside sources during scanning,
e.g. MR suite door left open with outside radio
signals entering, electronic/anesthesia devices in
the MR room, or inadequate/failure of RF shielding
(Faraday cage). Therefore, the frequency-encoding
direction in this image is in the X-dimension.

question_text: Which direction is the artifact?
choices: D. Both X and Y dimensions
explanation:
Ghosting and smearing artifacts are produced by
patient motion, both voluntary (e.g., patient
movement) and involuntary (in this case: eye
motion). It typically is most apparent in the phase-
encoding direction. Simply remember: only
chemical shift artifact is in the frequency-encoding
direction.

question_text: What type of imaging is this?
explanation:
In-phase (IP) and out-of-phase (OOP) sequences
correspond to paired MRI gradient echo (GRE)
sequences obtained with the same repetition time
(TR) but with two different echo time (TE) values.
Look for India ink artifact (chemical shift type 2),
black line outlining fat-water interfaces on out-of-
phase image. The main application of the IP-OOP
sequences is to identify microscopic fat content of
tissues in the abdomen by showing signal
intensities drop on the OOP images compared to
the IP images. In-phase and out-of-phase
conditions occur twice per cycle, or approximately
every 2.2 msec at 1.5T. (At 3.0T the phase cycling
is twice as fast, occurring every 1.1 msec). After an
excitation pulse on a 1.5T magnet, hydrogen atoms
in water and fat are in-phase at 0, 4.4, 8.8, 13.2 ms,
etc. Water and fat are out-of-phase at 0, 2.2, 6.6,
11. ms, etc. For a 3T magnet, water and fat are in-
phase at 0, 2.2, 4.4, 6.6 ms, etc. Water and fat are
out-of-phase at 0, 1.1, 3.3, 5.5 ms, etc. In other
words, the times at which hydrogen atoms in fat and
water are in-phase or out-of-phase is dependent on
the magnetic field strength (1.5 vs 3.0T).

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Herringbone artifact is a bad data point in K-space
caused by an electromagnetic spike by gradient
coils, power supply, etc. A single data point in K-
space contains information for all of k-space. When
converted to sinusoidal function, a bad data point
results in periodic bands repeating across the
image.

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Gibbs artifacts typically appear as multiple, periodic
high and low intensity fine lines radiating from high-
contrast interfaces at diminishing intensity. They
occur as a consequence of using Fourier transforms
to reconstruct images from MR signals with limited
sampling at high contrast interfaces/edges. The
high-frequency signals are "cuff-off," resulting in
distortion at high contrast borders. The artifact
occurs in phase-encoding direction, which by
default has fewer samples in order to shorten
imaging time. Remove the artifact by decreasing
pixel size (decrease FOV or increase matrix) or
swap phase & frequency-encoding directions.

question_text: Which sequence is shown?
explanation:
With India ink artifact (chemical shift type 2), a black
line is seen in all directions at fat-water interfaces.
In pixels with roughly equal amounts of fat and
water, the fat and water spins are 180-degree out of
phase at certain echo times because of their
chemical shift or frequency difference causing
cancellation of signal.

question_text: Which direction is the frequency-encoding direction?
choices: D. Both X and Y dimensions
explanation:
The image demonstrates chemical shift artifact
(type 1), which is a "mismapping/misregistration"
artifact. There's difference in resonant frequency of
water and fat, a chemical shift of 3.5 ppm or 224 Hz
at 1.5T. At the water/fat interfaces, fat protons in a
voxel containing both fat and water are mismapped
to a location lower in the frequency gradient
(frequency-encoding direction), and show up as a
bright band on one side and a dark band on the
other side of a fat-water interface. The image shows
a mismapped kidney along the X-dimension with a
black band along renal cortex at midpole. Chemical
shift is seen in spin-echo imaging with strong effect
at higher magnetic field strength. (Simply
remember: only chemical shift artifact is in the
frequency-encoding direction).

question_text: Which sequence is shown?
explanation:
At the bottom left corner of the image, notice that
TR: 1800, TE: 8.85, consistent with proton density
sequence. T1 sequence = Short TR (s 500 ms) and
Short TE (<20 ms). T2 = Long TR (22000 ms) and
Long TE (= 70 ms). PD sequence = Long TR (2000-
3000 ms) and Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text: Which sequence is shown?
explanation:
Notice how CSF is suppressed on this image,
compatible with fluid attenuation inversion recovery
(FLAIR) sequence. There are multiple characteristic
periventricular and pericallosal demyelinating
plagues in this patient with MS.

question_text: Which sequence is shown in the picture on the right?
explanation:
Increased signal on diffusion weighted imaging
(DWI, the left picture) can either represent an acute
infarct or T2 shine-through. ADC (right picture)
images are helpful for differentiating T2 shine-
through from acute infarct.

question_text: Which sequence is shown in the picture on the left?
explanation:
Left image: diffusion weighted imaging (DWI). Right
image: ADC. Notice how the spleen normally
restricts diffusion.

question_text: Which sequence is shown?
explanation:
At the bottom left corner of the image, notice that
TR: 9.86, TE: 4.59, consistent with T1 sequence. T1
sequence = Short TR (< 500 ms) and Short TE (<20
ms). T2 = Long TR (22000 ms) and Long TE (= 70
ms). PD sequence = Long TR (2000-3000 ms) and
Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text: Which sequence is shown?
explanation:
At the bottom left corner of the image, notice that
TR: 3000, TE: 80, consistent with T2 sequence. T1
sequence = Short TR (< 500 ms) and Short TE (<20
ms). T2 = Long TR (22000 ms) and Long TE (= 70
ms). PD sequence = Long TR (2000-3000 ms) and
Short TE (25-30 ms).

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Recognize that this is phase-contrast MR imaging
typically used in cardiac (in this case) or CSF flow
studies. Before MRI data are acquired, the
anticipated maximum flow velocity must be entered
into the velocity encoding (VENC) pulse sequence
protocol. To obtain the optimal signal, the flow
velocity should be the same as, or slightly less than,
the selected VENC. Flow velocities greater than
selected VENC can produce aliasing artifacts (in
this case), whereas velocities much smaller than
VENC result in a weak signal.

question_text:
Which image was reconstructed using a bone
algorithm/kernel?
explanation:
Both images are in soft tissue window. A soft tissue
reconstruction algorithm (Left image) decreases
image noise leading to increased contrast
resolution and imaging smoothing, which is more
pleasing to the eye. Image smoothing decreases
spatial resolution. Alternatively, a bone
filter/sharpening algorithm (Right image) increases
spatial resolution at the expense of increased noise
(decreased contrast resolution). Image
reconstruction from raw data using different
algorithms/kernels occurs after the scan is complete
and therefore have NO impact on patient dose.

question_text:
Which image was reconstructed using a soft tissue
algorithm/kernel?
explanation:
Both images are in bone window. A soft tissue
reconstruction algorithm (Left image) decreases
image noise leading to increased contrast
resolution and imaging smoothing, which is more
pleasing to the eye. Image smoothing decreases
spatial resolution. Alternatively, a bone
filter/sharpening algorithm (Right image) increases
spatial resolution at the expense of increased noise
(decreased contrast resolution). Image
reconstruction from raw data using different
algorithms/kernels occurs after the scan is complete
and therefore have NO impact on patient dose.

question_text: What is the artifact shown?
explanation:
Perivascular vibration artifact is a Doppler's bruit. It
appears as multiple tiny points or "spray" of color in
the perivascular tissues, caused by soft-tissue
vibration related to turbulent intravascular blood
flow. It is most commonly seen in perivascular soft
tissues in the setting of an arteriovenous fistula or
severely stenotic artery with high-velocity turbulent
flow.

question_text: Which artifact is associated with the gallstones?
explanation:
Highly attenuating structures like stone, bone or air
prevent the ultrasound wave from passing. As a
